_meta.purpose: Coverage Finder applicability matrix. Maps an organization's profile to the IL AI statutes that apply and the minimum compliance actions each requires.

schema.appliesIf.always: If true, the rule applies to every organization regardless of inputs. Used for criminal floors like HB 4623.
schema.appliesIf.any: Array of conditions. The rule applies if any condition matches. Within a condition, all specified dimensions must match (AND).
schema.condition.industries: Array of industry IDs. User matches if profile.industry is in this list.
schema.condition.useCases: Array of use-case IDs. User matches if ANY of profile.useCases is in this list.
schema.condition.revenueAtLeast: Minimum revenue tier ID. User matches if profile.revenue tier >= this. Tiers: lt-25m < 25-500m < gte-500m. 'unsure' always matches.
schema.condition.providerType: Array of provider-type IDs (builder | deployer). User matches if profile.providerType is in the list, OR if profile.providerType is 'both'.
schema.condition.minorsAudience: Boolean. User matches if profile.minorsAudience matches this value, OR if profile.minorsAudience is 'unsure'.
schema.primaryRoles: Array of role IDs for whom this statute is most operationally relevant. Used to sort output.

useCases.label: AI in hiring or HR decisions
AI in clinical or mental-health settings
AI in lending, housing, or credit decisions
AI in K-12 or higher education
Building or training large AI models

minimumActions: AI cannot act as an independent therapist; only licensed humans can deliver mental-health treatment.
Disable any 'emotion detection' or affective-computing features in clinical workflows.
Designate a licensed clinician as accountable for every AI-assisted clinical tool you deploy.
Document patient consent and clinical-oversight protocols whenever AI is in the loop.
Audit every AI hiring or HR tool for disparate impact across protected classes.
Notify applicants and employees in writing whenever AI is used in hiring, promotion, discipline, or discharge decisions.
Eliminate ZIP codes (and other protected-class proxies) from AI training and decision inputs.
Document your bias-testing methodology and retain results for IDHR review.
Provide written notice to every applicant before AI analyzes their video interview.
Explain in plain language how the AI tool works and what characteristics it evaluates.
Obtain explicit written consent before any AI analysis takes place.
Offer applicants the option to opt out and request a human review instead.
Review existing performer, contributor, and likeness contracts for AI-cloning provisions.
Re-paper any non-compliant likeness contracts with explicit explanation of AI-cloning rights and the right to counsel.
Add a 'no AI cloning without separate written consent' clause to all new performer contracts.
Maintain a documented record of consent for any AI-generated voice or likeness use.
Get explicit written consent before any AI-generated voice, image, or likeness goes into commercial content.
Train marketing and creative teams on the IL Right-of-Publicity Act's AI amendments.
Document clear exceptions for news, documentary, and satire uses with legal-review sign-off.
Maintain provenance metadata for every AI-generated talent likeness in production.
Implement filters that prevent any AI tool from generating sexual imagery of minors (criminal exposure: Class 1 / Class 4 felony).
Train staff on recognition and reporting of AI-generated CSAM.
Configure customer-facing AI products with abuse-detection guardrails on input and output.
Document an incident-response procedure for AI-CSAM discovery, preservation, and law-enforcement reporting.
rules.note: Universal floor — applies regardless of industry, size, or use case. Criminal liability.

minimumActions: Adopt or adapt curriculum that teaches students to recognize deepfakes and AI-generated content.
Align district / institutional AI use policies with Illinois State Board of Education (ISBE) guidance as it is published.
Train educators on responsible AI use in classroom settings.
Document a school-level policy on which AI tools are permitted for student work.
Pending: begin drafting a public safety and child-protection plan in case SB 3261 advances after the May 15 third-reading deadline.
Pending: prepare AG-reporting and incident-reporting capability scoped to large chatbot ($25M+ revenue, 1M+ users) and frontier developer ($500M+ revenue) thresholds.
Pending: track co-sponsor and amendment activity through committee.
Pending: audit any companion-AI features for crisis-detection and user safeguards.
Pending: prepare 'I am not a human' machine-disclosure flows that show every session.
Pending: prepare parental-consent flows if minors can access companion-AI features.
Pending: track the OpenAI-backed catastrophe-liability framework (covers any system trained with $100M+ in compute).
Pending: begin drafting a critical-harm safety framework if your training spend approaches the threshold.
Pending: watch the OpenAI / Anthropic split — the liability-shield provision is the wedge issue and likely to be amended.
Pending: build internal incident-reporting capability for catastrophic AI risk events.
Pending: track ILCompute / public-cloud framework for any state-funded research access.
Pending: begin documenting human-review procedures for any automated decision system in public-employer contexts.
Pending: prepare impact assessments for ADS deployments. The bill stalled at the April 17 floor deadline but is expected to return in May.
